text:
COMPREHENSIVE TUMOR GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT
Foundation One CDx-Equivalent Panel (324 Genes)

============================================================
PATIENT & SPECIMEN INFORMATION
============================================================
Patient Name: Margaret Okonkwo
Date of Birth: [REDACTED]
Sex: Female
Ordering Physician: [Urologic Oncology / Medical Oncology]
Diagnosis (Submitted): Renal cell carcinoma, clear cell type, right kidney, with sarcomatoid differentiation
Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) core needle biopsy
Specimen Source: Right renal mass, CT-guided core needle biopsy (3 cores submitted)
Specimen Adequacy: Adequate; estimated tumor cellularity 65% (manual dissection performed to enrich tumor content prior to nucleic acid extraction)
Date of Biopsy: [See procedure date]
Date Specimen Received in Molecular Pathology: [See report date]
Date Report Issued: [See report date]
Accession Number: [LABORATORY ASSIGNED]
Sequencing Platform: Illumina-based next-generation sequencing, hybrid-capture methodology
Panel: 324-gene comprehensive solid tumor panel with whole-exome backbone for copy number analysis; additional microsatellite instability (MSI) and tumor mutational burden (TMB) assessment included

============================================================
QUALITY METRICS
============================================================
Mean sequencing depth (coding regions): 742x
Percent bases covered at >=100x: 98.7%
Nucleic acid quality: Acceptable (DIN 4.8 for DNA; partial degradation expected in FFPE material, library successfully constructed)
Tumor purity (computational estimate): 67% (concordant with pathology estimate of 65%)
Signal-to-noise ratio: Adequate for somatic variant calling
MSI assay: 39 loci analyzed; sufficient coverage at all loci
Note: All quality thresholds met. Results are reportable.

============================================================
SUMMARY OF CLINICALLY ACTIONABLE FINDINGS
============================================================

1. VHL p.R200* (c.598C>T) -- PATHOGENIC NONSENSE MUTATION
   Gene: VHL (von Hippel-Lindau tumor suppressor)
   Variant class: Nonsense (stop-gain)
   Variant allele frequency (VAF): 48%
   Zygosity: Hemizygous / effectively homozygous loss (biallelic inactivation confirmed by concurrent loss of heterozygosity [LOH] at chromosome 3p25.3; see copy number section)
   Clinical significance: Pathogenic. VHL is the canonical driver mutation in clear cell RCC, occurring in approximately 75-80% of sporadic ccRCC cases. Biallelic inactivation of VHL leads to constitutive stabilization of HIF-1alpha and HIF-2alpha transcription factors, driving upregulation of angiogenic (VEGF, PDGF) and metabolic target genes. This alteration confirms the diagnosis of clear cell RCC at the molecular level and explains the tumor's inherent sensitivity to VEGF pathway inhibitors. The p.R200* nonsense mutation has been previously reported in COSMIC and ClinVar as a pathogenic driver in ccRCC.
   Germline correlation: Germline VHL testing was performed (see germline section); no pathogenic germline VHL alteration identified. This mutation is interpreted as somatic.

2. PBRM1 p.Q1233* (c.3697C>T) -- PATHOGENIC NONSENSE MUTATION
   Gene: PBRM1 (Polybromo-1; SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex subunit)
   Variant class: Nonsense (stop-gain)
   Variant allele frequency (VAF): 42%
   Zygosity: Hemizygous / biallelic loss (concurrent LOH at chromosome 3p21.1; see copy number section)
   Clinical significance: Pathogenic. PBRM1 is the second most commonly mutated gene in ccRCC, altered in approximately 40-50% of cases. It encodes the BAF180 subunit of the PBAF chromatin remodeling complex, functioning as a tumor suppressor. Loss of PBRM1 has been associated with altered immune microenvironment signaling and, in some retrospective analyses of Opdivo-treated ccRCC patients, has been correlated with differential response to immune checkpoint inhibition (specifically, loss-of-function PBRM1 mutations have been associated with improved outcomes with anti-PD-1 therapy in some datasets). The co-occurrence of PBRM1 and VHL loss on the same chromosome 3p deletion event is a defining molecular feature of clear cell RCC.

3. SETD2 p.K1340fs (c.4018delA, exon 12 frameshift deletion) -- PATHOGENIC FRAMESHIFT
   Gene: SETD2 (SET domain containing 2, histone methyltransferase)
   Variant class: Frameshift deletion (1-bp deletion, exon 12)
   Variant allele frequency (VAF): 31%
   Zygosity: Hemizygous / biallelic loss (concurrent LOH at chromosome 3p21.31; see copy number section)
   Clinical significance: Pathogenic. SETD2 encodes the sole trimethylase responsible for H3K36me3 histone modification, a mark associated with active transcription and DNA mismatch repair fidelity. SETD2 is mutated in approximately 10-15% of ccRCC cases and is located on chromosome 3p, frequently co-deleted with VHL and PBRM1 in the same chromosomal event. Loss of SETD2 has been linked to impaired homologous recombination fidelity and, in some preclinical models, may confer sensitivity to PARP inhibition (investigational context). The relatively lower VAF (31%) compared to VHL (48%) and PBRM1 (42%) may reflect intratumoral heterogeneity or subclonal acquisition following the initial 3p deletion event.

4. TP53 p.R175H (c.524G>A) -- PATHOGENIC HOTSPOT MISSENSE
   Gene: TP53 (Tumor protein p53)
   Variant class: Hotspot missense (gain-of-function/dominant-negative)
   Variant allele frequency (VAF): 28%
   Zygosity: Heterozygous (no evidence of LOH at 17p13.1; retained wild-type allele)
   Clinical significance: Pathogenic. The p.R175H substitution is one of the most frequently observed and well-characterized TP53 hotspot mutations across human cancers. It results in a conformational change in the p53 DNA-binding domain, abolishing sequence-specific DNA binding while simultaneously conferring oncogenic gain-of-function properties (promotion of cell survival, invasion, and resistance to apoptosis). In the context of ccRCC, TP53 mutations are less common (~5-10%) but enriched in high-grade and sarcomatoid tumors. The association of TP53 p.R175H with the sarcomatoid component in this tumor is biologically plausible, as TP53 pathway disruption is one of several molecular events implicated in epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and sarcomatoid dedifferentiation. VAF of 28% suggests this may represent a subclonal event acquired within the sarcomatoid subpopulation, though spatial heterogeneity cannot be excluded with a single biopsy specimen. Germline TP53 testing not indicated in this clinical context (patient is 63 years old, no clinical features of Li-Fraumeni syndrome); however, germline TP53 analysis is available upon request.

5. MET -- HIGH-LEVEL AMPLIFICATION
   Gene: MET (MET proto-oncogene, receptor tyrosine kinase)
   Alteration type: Copy number amplification
   Copy number (estimated): 8.2 copies (relative to ploidy-adjusted diploid baseline)
   Classification: High-level amplification (focal, chromosome 7q31.2)
   Clinical significance: Pathogenic / Potentially Actionable. High-level MET amplification (>=6 copies, focal) is a recognized oncogenic driver event in multiple tumor types and has been reported in approximately 3-5% of ccRCC cases, with enrichment in tumors with sarcomatoid features. MET encodes the receptor tyrosine kinase for hepatocyte growth factor (HGF). Amplification leads to ligand-independent receptor activation and signaling through downstream RAS/MAPK, PI3K/AKT, and STAT3 pathways, promoting invasion, metastasis, and angiogenesis. THERAPEUTIC RELEVANCE: Cabozantinib is a multi-kinase inhibitor with potent activity against both VEGFR2 and MET (as well as AXL, RET, and FLT3), and is approved for advanced RCC. Preclinical and retrospective clinical data suggest that MET-amplified RCC tumors may derive enhanced benefit from cabozantinib relative to pure VEGFR inhibitors (e.g., suni, pazo). This amplification is of particular therapeutic interest in the event of progression on first-line therapy.

============================================================
ADDITIONAL SOMATIC ALTERATIONS OF UNCERTAIN OR SECONDARY SIGNIFICANCE
============================================================

6. KDM5C p.D870G (c.2609A>G) -- VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (VUS)
   Gene: KDM5C (Lysine demethylase 5C; X-linked)
   VAF: 89% (high VAF consistent with hemizygous X-linked gene in a female patient [one X-chromosome]; however, this patient is female and carries two X chromosomes -- the high VAF of 89% suggests either loss of the wild-type allele on the second X chromosome or preferential allele expression; see note)
   Note on zygosity: KDM5C is an X-linked gene. In female patients, a VAF approaching 90% from a single missense alteration suggests either loss of the normal allele (LOH at Xp11.22) or extreme allelic imbalance, which is consistent with tumor-associated X-chromosome inactivation skewing commonly observed in RCC. The p.D870G substitution is a missense change in the JmjC demethylase catalytic domain; functional impact uncertain.
   Clinical significance: VUS. KDM5C is a histone H3K4 demethylase that functions as a tumor suppressor in RCC. Somatic mutations are reported in ~10-15% of ccRCC cases. The specific p.D870G substitution has limited published functional data. No approved targeted therapies directly targeting KDM5C.

7. ARID1A p.R1989W (c.5965C>T) -- VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (VUS)
   Gene: ARID1A (AT-rich interaction domain 1A; SWI/SNF complex)
   VAF: 22%
   Note: Low VAF consistent with subclonal acquisition. ARID1A encodes the BAF250a subunit of the canonical SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex. Missense alterations in ARID1A are commonly observed across many solid tumors; frameshift and nonsense mutations are typically pathogenic, while missense variants require case-by-case interpretation. The p.R1989W substitution is not a recurrent hotspot and its functional impact is uncertain.
   Clinical significance: VUS. No actionable therapeutic implications at this time.

8. MTOR p.L1460P (c.4379T>C) -- ACTIVATING MISSENSE (SUBCLONAL); VUS
   Gene: MTOR (Mechanistic target of rapamycin kinase)
   VAF: 19%
   Classification: Likely activating based on location within the FKBP12-rapamycin binding (FRB) domain; subclonal
   Clinical significance: This variant is located in a region of mTOR known to harbor activating mutations (FRB domain). mTOR activating mutations have been reported in approximately 5% of ccRCC cases and have been associated with sensitivity to mTOR inhibitors (e.g., evero, temsirolimus) in retrospective cohorts. However, the low VAF (19%) indicates this is a subclonal event and is unlikely to represent a dominant driver in this tumor. The p.L1460P substitution has limited published functional data in the context of ccRCC. If the tumor were to progress through VEGFR/MET inhibition, the presence of this subclonal MTOR-activating event could be considered in the context of clinical trial eligibility for mTOR pathway inhibitors.

9. BAP1 -- WILD-TYPE (diploid; no mutation detected)
   Gene: BAP1 (BRCA1-associated protein 1)
   Note: BAP1 is a critical tumor suppressor in ccRCC (mutated in ~10-15% of cases), also located on chromosome 3p21.1. Notably, PBRM1 and BAP1 mutations tend to be mutually exclusive in ccRCC (inverse correlation), and BAP1 wild-type status in this tumor is consistent with this pattern. The 3p deletion in this tumor has resulted in biallelic loss of VHL, PBRM1, and SETD2 but has spared BAP1, consistent with a deletion that extends from 3p25 (VHL locus) through 3p21.1 (PBRM1, SETD2) but does not encompass the BAP1 locus at 3p21.1 (a deletion boundary effect). Clinically, BAP1 wild-type status is associated with more favorable outcomes in ccRCC compared to BAP1-mutated cases.

============================================================
COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS
============================================================
Chromosome 3p -- LOSS (LOH)
  Region: 3p25.3 to 3p21.1 (large interstitial/terminal deletion)
  Genes impacted: VHL (3p25.3), PBRM1 (3p21.3), SETD2 (3p21.3)
  Note: BAP1 (3p21.1) retained at diploid copy number, consistent with proximal boundary of deletion
  Significance: This chromosome 3p loss is the canonical initiating event in ccRCC pathogenesis. When combined with the identified nonsense mutations in VHL and PBRM1 and the frameshift in SETD2 on the remaining allele, biallelic inactivation of all three tumor suppressors is confirmed.

Chromosome 5q -- GAIN
  Region: Chromosome 5q (arm-level)
  Significance: 5q gain is one of the most common secondary copy number events in ccRCC (~70% of tumors), typically involving genes such as NSD1 and SQSTM1. Exact therapeutic implications are uncertain but this is a commonly observed ccRCC copy number background event.

Chromosome 14q -- LOSS
  Region: Chromosome 14q (arm-level)
  Genes of interest: HIF1A (14q23.2), MAX (14q23.3)
  Significance: 14q loss occurs in approximately 45% of ccRCC cases and has been associated with higher nuclear grade, sarcomatoid differentiation, and adverse prognosis in some series. Loss of HIF1A at this locus creates reliance on HIF-2alpha-driven transcription, which has implications for HIF-2alpha-targeted therapy (belzutifan and similar agents).

Chromosome 7q31.2 (MET locus) -- HIGH-LEVEL FOCAL AMPLIFICATION
  Estimated copy number: 8.2
  Significance: High-level focal MET amplification (see actionable findings above).

Other chromosomal aberrations: No other high-level amplifications or homozygous deletions in genes of primary clinical actionability. Genome-wide fraction of altered copy number: 38% (moderate chromosomal instability).

============================================================
GENE FUSION ANALYSIS
============================================================
Fusions detected: NONE
Note: No clinically significant gene fusions were identified across the 324-gene panel. Specifically, no ALK, RET, NTRK1/2/3, ROS1, or other targetable fusions were detected. No TFE3 or TFEB fusion transcripts identified (consistent with IHC negativity for TFE3 reported in pathology). No VHL rearrangements. Fusion analysis is limited to the genes and fusion partners covered by this panel; uncommon or novel fusions not represented in the panel may not be detected.

============================================================
MICROSATELLITE INSTABILITY (MSI) STATUS
============================================================
Result: MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
Method: Fragment-length analysis of 39 microsatellite loci with comparison to matched normal tissue (blood-derived normal); confirmatory by next-generation sequencing-based analysis
Interpretation: MSS status is observed in the vast majority (>97%) of sporadic RCC. This result does not support use of MSI-H as a predictive biomarker for immune checkpoint inhibitor response in this patient, nor eligibility for pembro based on MSI-H indication alone. However, PD-L1 expression and tumor histology remain relevant for immunotherapy decision-making (see below).

============================================================
TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN (TMB)
============================================================
Result: LOW -- 3.1 mutations per megabase (mut/Mb)
Classification: TMB-Low (<10 mut/Mb)
Method: Calculation based on somatic non-synonymous mutations per megabase of coding sequence, excluding known driver mutations, germline variants, and copy number alterations
Interpretation: The TMB is low, consistent with the expected molecular profile of ccRCC, which is generally a tumor with low TMB due to the absence of hypermutator phenotypes (no POLE/POLD1 mutations detected; MSS). TMB-Low status does not support use of Keytruda for the TMB-High indication (FDA-approved for TMB >=10 mut/Mb, tumor agnostic). This result is concordant with the MSS finding above.

============================================================
PD-L1 EXPRESSION
============================================================
Result: PD-L1 Combined Positive Score (CPS) = 12
Assay: 22C3 pharmDx antibody (Dako/Agilent), performed on the same FFPE biopsy specimen
Interpretation: CPS of 12 indicates moderate PD-L1 expression. In the context of clear cell RCC, PD-L1 expression by CPS is not used as a strict inclusion/exclusion criterion for first-line immune checkpoint inhibitor combinations (e.g., Yervoy/nivo in CheckMate 214 was not restricted by PD-L1 status; Opdivo/cabozantinib and Keytruda/axitinib benefit was observed across PD-L1 subgroups). However, PD-L1 CPS 12, combined with the sarcomatoid histology and IMDC intermediate-risk classification, supports consideration of IO-based combination therapy in the first-line setting.

============================================================
GERMLINE FINDINGS
============================================================
Germline analysis was performed on matched peripheral blood mononuclear cells. The following hereditary RCC susceptibility genes were analyzed: VHL, FLCN, MET, SDHB, SDHC, SDHD, PTEN, FH, TSC1, TSC2.

Result: NO PATHOGENIC OR LIKELY PATHOGENIC GERMLINE ALTERATIONS DETECTED in any of the analyzed genes.

Specific findings:
- VHL (germline): No pathogenic variant. The somatic VHL p.R200* mutation identified in tumor DNA was NOT detected in matched germline (blood) DNA, confirming its somatic origin. This result effectively excludes hereditary Von Hippel-Lindau disease.
- FLCN (germline): No pathogenic variant (Birt-Hogg-Dube syndrome excluded)
- MET (germline): No pathogenic variant (Hereditary papillary RCC Type 1 excluded)
- SDHB/C/D (germline): No pathogenic variants (SDH-deficient RCC excluded)
- PTEN (germline): No pathogenic variant (Cowden syndrome excluded)

Note: The absence of a germline etiology, combined with the patient's age of onset (63 years), unilateral tumor, and no family history of RCC or associated syndromic features, is consistent with sporadic ccRCC.

Genetic counseling referral: Not indicated based on these results; however, referral may be considered if family history review reveals additional concerning personal or family history features.

============================================================
MOLECULAR PROFILE SUMMARY AND THERAPEUTIC CONTEXT
============================================================
Molecular Subtype: This tumor demonstrates the canonical ccRCC molecular profile: biallelic VHL inactivation (somatic nonsense + 3p LOH) as the founding driver event, with co-occurring biallelic inactivation of PBRM1 and SETD2 (also on 3p), consistent with a single-hit chromosomal deletion followed by additional mutations. The presence of a PBRM1-mutated / BAP1-wild-type profile places this tumor in the molecular subgroup historically associated with better outcomes compared to BAP1-mutated ccRCC, though the co-occurring high-grade features (TP53 p.R175H, MET amplification, sarcomatoid differentiation, Fuhrman grade 4, 14q loss) indicate aggressive tumor biology that overrides this more favorable molecular baseline.

Key Therapeutic Implications:

a) VEGFR Pathway: Biallelic VHL loss is the canonical mechanism driving HIF-alpha/VEGF pathway upregulation, supporting the expected sensitivity to VEGFR-directed therapies (cabozantinib, suni, pazo, axitinib, Lenvima). 14q loss with potential HIF1A hemizygous deletion may shift HIF-dependency toward HIF-2alpha, which is the primary target of belzutifan (HIF-2alpha inhibitor, FDA-approved for VHL disease-associated tumors and for relapsed/refractory ccRCC). Clinical trial eligibility for belzutifan or HIF-2alpha inhibitor combinations should be considered in the event of treatment resistance.

b) MET Pathway: High-level focal MET amplification (8.2 copies) is the most clinically distinctive alteration in this tumor. Cabozantinib (dual VEGFR2/MET inhibitor) is approved for advanced RCC and offers concurrent inhibition of both canonical (VHL/VEGF) and MET-driven oncogenic signaling. This molecular finding provides a strong scientific rationale for prioritizing cabozantinib over pure VEGFR inhibitors (Sutent, Votrient) in the event of treatment selection where a TKI is indicated. Clinical retrospective data (including METEOR trial subgroup analyses) suggest MET pathway activation correlates with cabozantinib benefit.

c) Immune Checkpoint Inhibition: PD-L1 CPS 12, MSS, and TMB-Low profile. Despite the low TMB-MSS context (which is generally associated with reduced response to single-agent checkpoint inhibition), dual checkpoint blockade (ipi/Opdivo) has demonstrated robust efficacy in ccRCC regardless of TMB/MSS status, particularly in the sarcomatoid subgroup (CheckMate 214 pre-specified analysis: ORR ~57% in sarcomatoid ccRCC with ipi/Opdivo). The combination of PD-L1 CPS 12, sarcomatoid differentiation (20%), and IMDC intermediate risk status strongly support an IO-based combination regimen as first-line therapy.

d) mTOR Pathway: Subclonal activating MTOR p.L1460P (VAF 19%) may represent a minor subpopulation with potential sensitivity to mTOR inhibitors (evero). This is a subclonal event of secondary importance at this stage and would be most relevant if primary VEGFR/MET-directed therapies fail.

e) SWI/SNF Complex: Loss of PBRM1 and ARID1A (VUS) suggest broad disruption of SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling. While no approved targeted therapies directly exploit SWI/SNF loss in RCC, this profile may confer synthetic lethality vulnerabilities (e.g., EZH2 inhibition, HDAC inhibition) that are being investigated in early-phase trials.

f) HIF-2alpha Inhibition: 14q loss affecting the HIF1A locus, combined with VHL biallelic loss, supports a HIF-2alpha-dominant transcriptional program. Belzutifan (PT2977/MK-6482) targets HIF-2alpha directly and is FDA-approved for VHL-disease-associated RCC and for relapsed/refractory ccRCC (LITESPARK-005 data); this agent should be considered as a future therapeutic option in subsequent lines.

============================================================
INTERPRETING PATHOLOGIST / MOLECULAR TUMOR BOARD ATTESTATION
============================================================
This report was reviewed and signed out by the Molecular Pathology Division. Results were reviewed in the context of the submitted clinical diagnosis and pathology report. The genomic findings are consistent with sporadic clear cell renal cell carcinoma, Fuhrman grade 4, with molecular features indicative of aggressive biology (TP53 hotspot, MET amplification, sarcomatoid association, 14q loss). All identified variants were assessed using current evidence-based databases (OncoKB, ClinVar, COSMIC, CIViC). This report reflects the mutational profile of the submitted specimen only; intratumoral heterogeneity may result in additional alterations not captured in this biopsy.

This genomic profile is recommended for correlation with clinical, pathological, and radiographic findings at multidisciplinary tumor board review.

[End of Report]
